title: Cat Vomiting Yellow Bile: Causes, Urgency, and Triage Guide
meta_desc: Is your cat vomiting yellow liquid? Learn why it happens, why the urgency is medium, and how a photo can help your vet.
content_html:
TL;DR: Yellow bile vomiting in cats usually indicates an empty stomach, but requires veterinary attention if it persists for more than 12-24 hours or is accompanied by lethargy and loss of appetite.

What does it mean when my cat vomits yellow bile?

Yellow bile, or gastric juice, is a digestive fluid produced by the liver. When your cat vomits yellow liquid, it usually indicates that their stomach is empty. The color comes from bile that has backed up from the small intestine into the stomach. This can be caused by long gaps between feedings, hairballs, or underlying irritation in the digestive tract.

Is my cat vomiting yellow bile a medical emergency?

  • The urgency level for repeated yellow vomiting is typically Medium, but increases to High if your cat cannot keep water down.
  • While a single episode may be minor, repeated vomiting can quickly lead to dangerous dehydration.
  • Contact a veterinarian if the vomiting continues for more than 12-24 hours.
  • Seek professional help if your cat seems lethargic, refuses food, or has a painful abdomen.

Why should I take a photo of my cat's vomit for the vet?

  • A photo allows the medical team to assess the volume and the specific shade of yellow.
  • It helps the vet identify the consistency, such as the presence of foam or mucus.
  • Visual cues help distinguish between a simple upset stomach and serious issues like physical obstructions or inflammatory bowel disease.

Clinical Context (Merck Veterinary Manual)

Vomiting yellow bile in cats may indicate extrahepatic biliary duct obstruction (EHBDO). Bile stasis can occur due to obstruction of the cystic or hepatic ducts, leading to bile dehydration and potentially the formation of a gallbladder mucocele or cholelith precipitation. In cases of EHBDO secondary to pancreatitis, cats may experience greater morbidity with biliary stent placement compared to dogs. Surgical exploration of biliary structures is warranted; patency of the cystic and common bile ducts must be determined, and viability of the gallbladder ascertained. Cholecystectomy is often the treatment of choice, though cholecystoenterostomy or choledochoenterostomy may be considered to circumvent a permanently occluded distal common bile duct.

Chapter: Gastroenterology, Hepatology

Source: The Merck Veterinary Manual, 11th Edition (Page 479)

faqs.question: Can I give my cat anything for vomiting at home?
faqs.answer: You should never give human medications to a cat without a vet's approval. A brief fast of 2-4 hours followed by small amounts of water or a bland diet may be recommended after consulting your vet.

faqs.question: Is yellow vomit different from white foam?
faqs.answer: Yellow vomit specifically contains bile from the gallbladder, whereas white foam is often just saliva and gastric acid. Both usually indicate an empty stomach, but persistent yellow bile is often a sign of more significant nausea.
